is the traditional [prestige variety](/wiki/Prestige_variety "Prestige variety") of [Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese"), a [Sinitic language](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages") belonging to the [Sino-Tibetan language family](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_language_family "Sino-Tibetan language family"). It originated in the city of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") ([formerly romanized](/wiki/Chinese_postal_romanization "Chinese postal romanization") as Canton) and its surrounding [Pearl River Delta](/wiki/Pearl_River_Delta "Pearl River Delta").[[1]](#cite_note-Snow-3) Although *Cantonese* specifically refers to the prestige variety in [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), the term is often used more broadly to describe the entire Yue subgroup of Chinese, including varieties such as [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese"), which have limited [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with Cantonese.

**Cantonese** is the traditional [prestige variety](/wiki/Prestige_variety "Prestige variety") of [Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese"), a [Sinitic language](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages") belonging to the [Sino-Tibetan language family](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_language_family "Sino-Tibetan language family"). It originated in the city of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") ([formerly romanized](/wiki/Chinese_postal_romanization "Chinese postal romanization") as Canton) and its surrounding [Pearl River Delta](/wiki/Pearl_River_Delta "Pearl River Delta").[[1]](#cite_note-Snow-3) Although *Cantonese* specifically refers to the prestige variety in [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), the term is often used more broadly to describe the entire Yue subgroup of Chinese, including varieties such as [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese"), which have limited [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with Cantonese.

Cantonese is viewed as a vital and inseparable part of the cultural identity for its [native speakers](/wiki/Cantonese_people "Cantonese people") across large swaths of [southeastern China](/wiki/South_China "South China"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), as well as in [overseas communities](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese"). In [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), it is the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of the province of [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") (being the majority language of the Pearl River Delta) and neighbouring areas such as [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"). It is also the dominant and co-official language of Hong Kong and Macau. Further, Cantonese is widely spoken among [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") (most notably in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), as well as in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") and [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") to a lesser extent) and the Western world.[[1]](#cite_note-Snow-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-4) With about 80 million total speakers as of 2023, standard Cantonese is by far the most spoken variant of Yue Chinese and non-Mandarin Chinese language.[[3]](#cite_note-5)

Although Cantonese shares much vocabulary with [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") and other [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"), these [Sinitic languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages") have very limited to no [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible"), largely because of phonological differences, but also differences in grammar and vocabulary. Sentence structure, in particular the verb placement, sometimes differs between the two varieties. A notable difference between Cantonese and Mandarin is how the spoken word is written; both can be recorded verbatim, but very few Cantonese speakers are knowledgeable in the full Cantonese written vocabulary, so a non-verbatim formalized written form is adopted, which is more akin to the written [Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthewsYip19945-6)[[1]](#cite_note-Snow-3) However, it is only non-verbatim with respect to vernacular Cantonese as it is possible to read Standard Chinese text verbatim in formal Cantonese, often with only slight changes in lexicon that are optional depending on the reader's choice of register.[[5]](#cite_note-7) This results in a situation in which a Cantonese and a Mandarin text may look similar but are pronounced differently. Conversely, written (vernacular) Cantonese is mostly used in informal settings like social media and comic books.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthewsYip19945-6)[[1]](#cite_note-Snow-3)

## Names of Cantonese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Names of Cantonese")]

In English, the term "Cantonese" can be ambiguous. "Cantonese" as used to refer to the language native to the city of Canton, which is the traditional English name of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), was popularized by *An English and Cantonese Pocket Dictionary* (1859), a bestseller by the missionary [John Chalmers](/wiki/John_Chalmers_(missionary) "John Chalmers (missionary)").[[6]](#cite_note-kataoka-8) Before 1859, this variant was often referred to in English as "the Canton dialect".[[7]](#cite_note-9)[[6]](#cite_note-kataoka-8)

However, "Cantonese" may also refer to the primary branch of Chinese that contains Cantonese proper as well as [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese") and [Gaoyang](/wiki/Gao-Yang_Yue "Gao-Yang Yue"); this broader usage may be specified as "[Yue speech](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese")" (粵語; 粤语; *Jyut6 jyu5*; *Yuhtyúh*). In this article, "Cantonese" is used for Cantonese proper (Guangzhou).

Historically, speakers called this variety "Guangzhou speech" (廣州話; 广州话; *Gwong2 zau1 waa2*; *Gwóngjāu wá*), although this term is now seldom used outside mainland China. In Guangdong and Guangxi, people also call it "provincial capital speech" (省城話; 省城话; *Saang2 seng4 waa2*; *Sáangsèhng wá*) or "plain speech" (白話; 白话; *Baak6 waa2*; *Baahkwá*). In academic linguistic circles, it is also referred to as "Guangzhou prefecture speech" (廣府話; 广府话; *Gwong2 fu2 waa2*; *Gwóngfú wá*).[[8]](#cite_note-Booney_1854-10)

In Hong Kong and Macau, as well as among overseas Chinese communities, the language is referred to as "Guangdong speech" or "Canton Province Speech" (廣東話; 广东话; *Gwong2 dung1 waa2*; *Gwóngdūng wá*) or simply as "Chinese" (中文; *Zung1 man2*; *Jūngmán*).[[9]](#cite_note-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Tangyun_-_Chinese_Dictionary_Museum.JPG/250px-Tangyun_-_Chinese_Dictionary_Museum.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tangyun_-_Chinese_Dictionary_Museum.JPG)

Chinese dictionary from the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"). Modern Cantonese pronunciation preserves almost all terminal consonants (-m, -n, -ng, -p, -t, -k) from Middle Chinese.

During the [Southern Song](/wiki/Song_Dynasty#Southern_Song,_1127–1279 "Song Dynasty") period, Guangzhou became the cultural center of the region.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYue-Hashimoto19724-13) Cantonese emerged as the [prestige variety](/wiki/Prestige_(sociolinguistics) "Prestige (sociolinguistics)") of [Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") when the port city of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") on the [Pearl River Delta](/wiki/Pearl_River_Delta "Pearl River Delta") became the largest port in China, with a trade network stretching as far as Arabia.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2006126-14) Specifically, the mutually intelligible speech of the *Sam Yap* (三邑), the Three Counties of Guangzhou, namely the historical counties of Panyu (番禺), [Nanhai](/wiki/Nanhai,_Foshan "Nanhai, Foshan") (南海), and [Shunde](/wiki/Shunde "Shunde") (顺德), came to be heralded as the standard.[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15) Cantonese was also used in the popular *Yuè'ōu*, *Mùyú* and *Nányīn* folksong genres, as well as [Cantonese opera](/wiki/Cantonese_opera "Cantonese opera").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYue-Hashimoto19725–6-16)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsey198799-17) Additionally, a distinct classical literature was developed in Cantonese, with [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") texts sounding more similar to modern Cantonese than other present-day Chinese varieties, including Mandarin.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYue-Hashimoto19725-18)

As Guangzhou became China's key commercial center for foreign trade and exchange in the 1700s, Cantonese became the variety of Chinese interacting most with the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2006126-14) Much of the sources for this period of early Cantonese, such as the 18th century [rime dictionary](/wiki/Rime_dictionary "Rime dictionary") *Fenyun Cuoyao* ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 分韻撮要; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Fēnyùn Cuòyào*; [Cantonese Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese"): *Fān Wáhn Chyutyiu*) and the 1828 *Vocabulary of the Canton Dialect* by the missionary [Robert Morrison](/wiki/Robert_Morrison_(missionary) "Robert Morrison (missionary)"), were written in Guangzhou during this period.[[17]](#cite_note-Sham2020-19)

After the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), centuries of [maritime prohibitions](/wiki/Haijin "Haijin") ended. Large numbers of Cantonese people from the Pearl River Delta, especially merchants, subsequently migrated by boat to other parts of Guangdong and Guangxi. These migrants established enclaves of Cantonese in areas that primarily spoke other forms of Yue or even non-Sinitic languages such as [Zhuang](/wiki/Zhuang_language "Zhuang language"), as with the case of the [Yong–Xun Yue](/wiki/Yong%E2%80%93Xun_Yue "Yong–Xun Yue") dialect of [Nanning](/wiki/Nanning "Nanning").[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15) Many Cantonese migrants sailed also overseas, bringing the Cantonese language with them to Southeast Asia, North and South America, and Western Europe. Such enclaves of Cantonese are still found in [Chinatowns](/wiki/Chinatowns "Chinatowns") across many of these major cities outside China.[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15) During the late 19th century, the pedagogical work *Cantonese made easy*, written by [James Dyer Ball](/wiki/James_Dyer_Ball "James Dyer Ball") in 1883, articulated the provenance of the prestige accent of Cantonese: that of the district of [Xiguan](/wiki/Xiguan "Xiguan") ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 西关; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 西關; [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *Sai1 Gwaan1*; [Cantonese Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese"): *Sāigwāan*) in the west of Guangzhou.[[18]](#cite_note-20) It is known for its distinctive use of an [apical vowel](/wiki/Apical_vowel "Apical vowel") (/ɿ/, or in more conventional [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet"): /ź̩~ɯ~ɨ/) in some cases where modern Cantonese would use a /i/ final.[[17]](#cite_note-Sham2020-19)

Throughout the 19th century and continuing into the 20th century, the ancestors of most of the population of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") arrived from Guangzhou and surrounding areas after they were ceded to [Britain](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), respectively.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYue-Hashimoto197270-21) The influx of such migrants into Hong Kong established Cantonese as the main language of the colony, supplanting local Yue Chinese varieties, which were closer to the dialects of neighboring [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") and [Dongguan](/wiki/Dongguan "Dongguan"), as well as the [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") and [Southern Min](/wiki/Southern_Min "Southern Min") varieties of the region.[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15) With subsequent waves of migration into Hong Kong, even as late as the 1950s, the proportion of native Cantonese speakers in Hong Kong had not yet surpassed 50%; nonetheless, this figure has risen to above 90% since the 1970s.[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15) On the other hand, the indigenous variety of Yue Chinese in Macau had been close to that of [Zhongshan](/wiki/Zhongshan "Zhongshan"), and this has had an effect on the tonal phonology of the Cantonese spoken in Macau.[[20]](#cite_note-22)[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15)

As a significant proportion of the entertainment industry in China migrated to Hong Kong in the early decades of the 20th century, the Hong Kong-based entertainment industry underwent a transformation to suit overseas as well as domestic audiences.[[21]](#cite_note-Bordwell2000-23) With the bifurcation of [the film industry](/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong") into Cantonese and Mandarin,[[21]](#cite_note-Bordwell2000-23) the use of the Xiguan accent of Guangzhou as a conservative prestige accent of standard Cantonese was maintained in mass media, with films from the 1930s making prominent use of it. However, during this time many phonological changes can be detected, indicating the change from Early Cantonese to Modern Cantonese.[[17]](#cite_note-Sham2020-19)

In mainland China, [Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") has been heavily promoted as the medium of instruction in schools and as the official language, especially after the communist takeover in 1949. Meanwhile, Cantonese has remained the official variety of Chinese in Hong Kong and Macau, both during and after the colonial period, under the policy of '[biliteracy and trilingualism](/wiki/Culture_of_Hong_Kong#Government_linguistic_policy "Culture of Hong Kong")' ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 兩文三語; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *liǎngwén sānyǔ*; [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"): *loeng3 man4 saam1 jyu5*).[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhangYang2004154-24) Government and law still function predominantly in Cantonese in these jurisdictions, and officials speak Cantonese even at the most formal occasions.[[13]](#cite_note-Sousa2022-15)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

### Hong Kong and Macau

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hong Kong and Macau")]

See also: [Hong Kong Cantonese](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cantonese "Hong Kong Cantonese")

The [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") are Chinese and English, as defined in the [Hong Kong Basic Law](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Basic_Law "Hong Kong Basic Law").[[23]](#cite_note-25) The Chinese language has [many different varieties](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"), of which Cantonese is one. Given the traditional predominance of Cantonese within Hong Kong, it is the *de facto* official spoken form of the Chinese language used in the [Hong Kong Government](/wiki/Government_of_Hong_Kong "Government of Hong Kong") and all courts and tribunals. It is also used as the [medium of instruction](/wiki/Medium_of_instruction "Medium of instruction") in schools, alongside English.

A similar situation also exists in neighbouring [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), where Chinese is an official language alongside [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"). As in Hong Kong, Cantonese is the predominant spoken [variety of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") used in everyday life and is thus the official form of Chinese used in the government. The Cantonese spoken in Hong Kong and Macau is [mutually intelligible](/wiki/Mutually_intelligible "Mutually intelligible") with the Cantonese spoken in the mainland city of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), although there exist some minor differences in accent, pronunciation, and vocabulary.

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Ping_and_Yue_dialect_map.svg/500px-Ping_and_Yue_dialect_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ping_and_Yue_dialect_map.svg)

Distribution of Yue Chinese languages in southeastern China. Standard Cantonese and closely related dialects are highlighted in pink.

Cantonese first developed around the port city of [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") in the [Pearl River Delta](/wiki/Pearl_River_Delta "Pearl River Delta") region of southeastern China. Due to the city's long standing role as an important cultural centre, Cantonese emerged as the prestige dialect of the [Yue varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") in the [Southern Song](/wiki/Southern_Song "Southern Song") dynasty and its usage spread around most of what is now the provinces of [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYue-Hashimoto19724-13)

Despite the cession of Macau to Portugal in 1557 and [Hong Kong to Britain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanking "Treaty of Nanking") in 1842, the ethnic Chinese population of the two territories largely originated from the 19th and 20th century immigration from Guangzhou and surrounding areas, making Cantonese the predominant Chinese language in the territories. On the mainland, Cantonese continued to serve as the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* of [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") and [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi") even after Mandarin was made the official language of the government by the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") in the early 1900s.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoblin2000549–550-26) Cantonese remained a dominant and influential language in southeastern China until the establishment of the People's Republic of China in 1949 and its promotion of [Standard Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") as the sole official language of the nation throughout the last half of the 20th century, although its influence still remains strong within the region.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsey19873–15-27)

While the Chinese government encourages the use of Standard Mandarin rather than local varieties of Chinese in broadcasts,[[26]](#cite_note-sarft-1-28) Cantonese enjoys a relatively higher standing than other Chinese languages, with its own media and usage in public transportation in Guangdong province. Furthermore, it is also a medium of instruction in select academic curricula, including some university elective courses and [Chinese as a foreign language](/wiki/Chinese_as_a_foreign_language "Chinese as a foreign language") programs.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30) The permitted usage of Cantonese in mainland China is largely a countermeasure against the influence from Hong Kong, which has historically enjoyed more media freedom than its former counterpart, as its Cantonese-language media has a substantial exposure and following in Guangdong.[[29]](#cite_note-31)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhangYang2004154-24)

Nevertheless, the place of local Cantonese language and [culture](/wiki/Cantonese_culture "Cantonese culture") remains contentious, as with other non-Mandarin Chinese languages.[[30]](#cite_note-32) A 2010 [proposal to switch some programming on Guangzhou television](/wiki/Guangzhou_Television_Cantonese_controversy "Guangzhou Television Cantonese controversy") from Cantonese to Mandarin was abandoned following massive public protests, the largest since the [Tiananmen Square protests of 1989](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square_protests_of_1989 "Tiananmen Square protests of 1989"). As a major economic centre of China, there have been concerns that the use of Cantonese in Guangzhou is diminishing in favour of Mandarin, both through the continual influx of Mandarin-speaking migrants from impoverished areas and strict government policies.[[31]](#cite_note-33) As a result, Cantonese is being given a more important status by the natives than ever before as a common identity of the local people.[[32]](#cite_note-34) This has led to initiatives to revive the language such as its introduction into school curricula and locally produced programs on broadcast media.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)

### Southeast Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Southeast Asia")]

Cantonese has historically served as a *lingua franca* among overseas Chinese in Southeast Asia, who speak a variety of other forms of Chinese including [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect"), and [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese").[[35]](#cite_note-37) Additionally, Cantonese media and popular culture such as [Hong Kong cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong") is popular throughout the region.

#### Vietnam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Vietnam")]

See also: [Hoa people](/wiki/Hoa_people "Hoa people")

In [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), Cantonese is the dominant language of the main ethnic Chinese community, usually referred to as *[Hoa](/wiki/Hoa_people "Hoa people")*, which numbers about one million people and constitutes one of the largest minority groups in the country.[[36]](#cite_note-GSO2019-38) Over half of the ethnic Chinese population in Vietnam speaks Cantonese as a native language and the variety also serves as a lingua franca between the different Chinese dialect groups.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhanh199331-39) Many speakers reflect their exposure to [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") with a Vietnamese accent or a tendency to [code-switch](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") between Cantonese and Vietnamese.

#### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

Main article: [Malaysian Cantonese](/wiki/Malaysian_Cantonese "Malaysian Cantonese")

See also: [Malaysian Chinese § Cantonese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese#Cantonese "Malaysian Chinese")

In [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), Cantonese is widely spoken among the [Malaysian Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") community in the capital city of [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur")[[38]](#cite_note-40) and the surrounding areas in the [Klang Valley](/wiki/Klang_Valley "Klang Valley") ([Petaling Jaya](/wiki/Petaling_Jaya "Petaling Jaya"), [Ampang](/wiki/Ampang,_Selangor "Ampang, Selangor"), [Putrajaya](/wiki/Putrajaya "Putrajaya"), [Cheras](/wiki/Cheras,_Selangor "Cheras, Selangor"), [Selayang](/wiki/Selayang "Selayang"), [Sungai Buloh](/wiki/Sungai_Buloh "Sungai Buloh"), [Puchong](/wiki/Puchong "Puchong"), [Shah Alam](/wiki/Shah_Alam "Shah Alam"), [Kajang](/wiki/Kajang "Kajang"), [Bangi](/wiki/Bangi,_Malaysia "Bangi, Malaysia"), and [Subang Jaya](/wiki/Subang_Jaya "Subang Jaya")). The language is also widely spoken as well in the town of [Sekinchan](/wiki/Sekinchan "Sekinchan") in the district of
[Sabak Bernam](/wiki/Sabak_Bernam "Sabak Bernam") located in the northern part of [Selangor](/wiki/Selangor "Selangor") state; the state of [Perak](/wiki/Perak "Perak"), especially in the state capital city of [Ipoh](/wiki/Ipoh "Ipoh") and its surrounding towns of [Gopeng](/wiki/Gopeng "Gopeng"), [Batu Gajah](/wiki/Batu_Gajah "Batu Gajah"), and [Kampar](/wiki/Kampar,_Perak "Kampar, Perak") of the [Kinta Valley](/wiki/Kinta_Valley "Kinta Valley") region plus the towns of [Tapah](/wiki/Tapah "Tapah") and [Bidor](/wiki/Bidor "Bidor") in the southern part of the [Perak](/wiki/Perak "Perak") state; and most of the state of [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan "Negeri Sembilan"), especially in the state capital city of [Seremban](/wiki/Seremban "Seremban"), as well as the towns of [Port Dickson](/wiki/Port_Dickson "Port Dickson"), [Kuala Pilah](/wiki/Kuala_Pilah "Kuala Pilah"), [Bahau](/wiki/Bahau "Bahau"), [Tampin](/wiki/Tampin "Tampin"), [Rembau](/wiki/Rembau_(town) "Rembau (town)") and [Gemas](/wiki/Gemas "Gemas"), with the exception of [Jelebu](/wiki/Jelebu "Jelebu"), [Mantin](/wiki/Mantin "Mantin"), [Nilai](/wiki/Nilai "Nilai") and [Kuala Klawang](/wiki/Kuala_Klawang "Kuala Klawang"), where [Hokkien](/wiki/Southern_Malaysian_Hokkien "Southern Malaysian Hokkien") and [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_language "Hakka language") predominate, respectively. Cantonese is also widely spoken in the eastern [Sabahan](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") town of [Sandakan](/wiki/Sandakan "Sandakan") as well as the towns of [Kuantan](/wiki/Kuantan "Kuantan"), [Raub](/wiki/Raub,_Pahang "Raub, Pahang"), [Bentong](/wiki/Bentong "Bentong"), [Temerloh](/wiki/Temerloh "Temerloh"), [Pekan](/wiki/Pekan,_Pahang "Pekan, Pahang"), [Jerantut](/wiki/Jerantut "Jerantut") as well as [Cameron Highlands](/wiki/Cameron_Highlands "Cameron Highlands") in Pahang state, and they are also found in other areas like [Sarikei](/wiki/Sarikei "Sarikei"), Sarawak, and [Mersing](/wiki/Mersing "Mersing"), Johor.

Although [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") is the most natively spoken variety of Chinese and Mandarin is the medium of education at [Chinese-language schools](/wiki/Chinese-language_schools "Chinese-language schools"), Cantonese is largely influential in the local Chinese media and is used in commerce by Chinese Malaysians.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

Due to the popularity of [Hong Kong popular culture](/wiki/Hong_Kong_popular_culture "Hong Kong popular culture"), especially through drama series and popular music, Cantonese is widely understood by the Chinese in all parts of Malaysia, even though a large proportion of the Chinese Malaysian population is non-Cantonese. Television networks in Malaysia regularly broadcast Hong Kong television programmes in their original Cantonese audio and soundtrack. Cantonese radio is also available in the nation and Cantonese is prevalent in locally produced Chinese television.[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43)

Cantonese spoken in Malaysia and Singapore often exhibits influences from [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") and other Chinese varieties spoken in the country, like Hokkien and Teochew.[[42]](#cite_note-44)

#### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Singapore")]

See also: [Chinese Singaporeans](/wiki/Chinese_Singaporeans "Chinese Singaporeans") and [Languages of Singapore](/wiki/Languages_of_Singapore "Languages of Singapore")

The Singapore government uses Mandarin as the official Chinese variety and has a [Speak Mandarin Campaign](/wiki/Speak_Mandarin_Campaign "Speak Mandarin Campaign") (SMC) seeking to actively promote using Mandarin at the expense of other Chinese varieties. A little over 15% of Chinese households in Singapore speak Cantonese. Despite the government actively promoting SMC, the Cantonese-speaking Chinese community has been relatively successful in preserving its language from Mandarin compared with other dialect groups.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

Notably, all nationally produced non-Mandarin Chinese TV and radio programs were stopped after 1979.[[44]](#cite_note-cna-46) The prime minister, [Lee Kuan Yew](/wiki/Lee_Kuan_Yew "Lee Kuan Yew"), then, also stopped giving speeches in [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") to prevent giving conflicting signals to the people.[[44]](#cite_note-cna-46) Hong Kong (Cantonese) and [Taiwanese](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien") dramas are unavailable in their untranslated form on free-to-air television, though drama series in non-Chinese languages are available in their original languages. Cantonese drama series on terrestrial TV channels are instead dubbed in Mandarin and broadcast without the original Cantonese audio and soundtrack. However, originals may be available through other sources like cable television and online videos.

Furthermore, an offshoot of SMC is the translation to [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin") of certain terms which originated from southern Chinese varieties. For instance, [dim sum](/wiki/Dim_sum "Dim sum") is often known as *diǎn xīn* in Singapore's English-language media, though this is largely a matter of style, and most Singaporeans will still refer to it as *dim sum* when speaking English.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

Nevertheless, since the government restriction on media in non-Mandarin varieties was relaxed in the mid-1990s and 2000s, presence of Cantonese in Singapore has grown substantially. Forms of popular culture from Hong Kong, like [television series](/wiki/Hong_Kong_television_drama "Hong Kong television drama"), [cinema](/wiki/Hong_Kong_cinema "Hong Kong cinema") and [pop music](/wiki/Cantopop "Cantopop") have become popular in Singaporean society, and non-dubbed original versions of the media became widely available. Consequently, the number of non-Cantonese Chinese Singaporeans being able to understand or speak Cantonese to some varying extent is growing, with a number of educational institutes offering Cantonese as an elective language course.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

#### Cambodia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cambodia")]

Cantonese is widely used as the inter-communal language among [Chinese Cambodians](/wiki/Chinese_Cambodian "Chinese Cambodian"), especially in [Phnom Penh](/wiki/Phnom_Penh "Phnom Penh") and other urban areas. While Teochew speakers form the majority of the Chinese population in [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), Cantonese is often used as a vernacular in commerce and with other Chinese variant groups in the nation.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Chinese-language schools in Cambodia are conducted in both Cantonese and Mandarin, but schools may be conducted exclusively in one Chinese variant or the other.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

#### Thailand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Thailand")]

While [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") is home to the [largest overseas Chinese community](/wiki/Thai_Chinese "Thai Chinese") in the world, the vast majority of ethnic Chinese in the country speak Thai exclusively.[[49]](#cite_note-51) Among Chinese-speaking Thai households, Cantonese is the fourth most-spoken Chinese variety after [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_language "Teochew language"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") and [Hainanese](/wiki/Hainanese "Hainanese").[[50]](#cite_note-52) Nevertheless, within the Thai Chinese commercial sector, it serves as a common language alongside Teochew or Thai. Chinese-language schools in Thailand have also traditionally been conducted in Cantonese. Furthermore, Cantonese serves as the lingua franca with other Chinese communities in the region.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

#### Indonesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Indonesia")]

In [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), Cantonese is locally known as *Konghu* and is one of the variants spoken by the [Chinese Indonesian](/wiki/Chinese_Indonesian "Chinese Indonesian") community, with speakers largely concentrated in certain major cities like [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), [Medan](/wiki/Medan "Medan"), [Surabaya](/wiki/Surabaya "Surabaya"), [Makassar](/wiki/Makassar "Makassar"), [Semarang](/wiki/Semarang "Semarang"), [Manado](/wiki/Manado "Manado") and [Batam](/wiki/Batam "Batam"). However, it has a relatively minor presence compared to other Southeast Asian nations, being the fourth most spoken Chinese variety after Hokkien, Hakka and Teochew.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis2005391-54)

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: North America")]

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Chinese language in the United States](/wiki/Chinese_language_in_the_United_States "Chinese language in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/San_Francisco_China_Town_MC.jpg/250px-San_Francisco_China_Town_MC.jpg)](/wiki/File:San_Francisco_China_Town_MC.jpg)

Street in [Chinatown, San Francisco](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco"). Cantonese has traditionally been the dominant Chinese variant among Chinese populations in the Western world.

Over a period of 150 years (from 1850 to the 2000s), Guangdong has been the place-of-origin for most Chinese emigrants to Western nations; one coastal county, [Taishan](/wiki/Taishan,_Guangdong "Taishan, Guangdong") (or Tóisàn, where the Sìyì or *sei yap* variety of Yue is spoken), alone may be the origin of the vast majority of Chinese immigrants to the U.S. before 1965.[[53]](#cite_note-55) As a result, Yue languages such as Cantonese and the closely related variety of [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese") have been the major Chinese varieties traditionally spoken in the United States. In 2009, 458,840 Americans spoke Cantonese at home according to an American Community Survey.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

The Zhongshan variant of Cantonese, which originated from the western Pearl River Delta, is spoken by many Chinese immigrants in Hawaii, and some in San Francisco and the [Sacramento River](/wiki/Sacramento_River "Sacramento River") Delta (see [Locke, California](/wiki/Locke,_California "Locke, California")). It is a Yuehai variety much like Guangzhou Cantonese but has "flatter" tones. Chinese is the second most widely spoken non-English language in the United States when both Cantonese and Mandarin are combined, behind Spanish.[[55]](#cite_note-57) Many institutes of higher education have traditionally had Chinese programs based on Cantonese, with some continuing to offer these programs despite the rise of Mandarin. The most popular romanization for learning Cantonese in the United States is [Yale romanization](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese").

The majority of Chinese emigrants have traditionally originated from Guangdong and Guangxi, as well as Hong Kong and Macau (beginning in the latter half of the 20th century and before the [handover](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong")) and Southeast Asia, with Cantonese as their native language. However, more recent immigrants are arriving from the rest of mainland China and Taiwan and most often speak [Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") (Putonghua) as their native language,[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) although some may also speak their native local variety, such as [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese"), [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), [Fuzhounese](/wiki/Fuzhounese "Fuzhounese"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_language "Hakka language"), etc. As a result, Mandarin is becoming more common among the Chinese American community.

The increase of Mandarin-speaking communities has resulted in the rise of separate neighbourhoods or enclaves segregated by the primary Chinese variety spoken. Socioeconomic statuses are also a factor.[[58]](#cite_note-Chee_Beng_Tan_2007_115-60) For example, in New York City, Cantonese still predominates in the city's [older, traditional western portion](/wiki/Mott_Street "Mott Street") of [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and in Brooklyn's small new Chinatowns in [Bensonhurst](/wiki/Bensonhurst,_Brooklyn "Bensonhurst, Brooklyn") and [Homecrest](/wiki/Homecrest,_Brooklyn "Homecrest, Brooklyn"). The newly emerged [Little Fuzhou](/wiki/Little_Fuzhou "Little Fuzhou") eastern portion of Manhattan's Chinatown and [Brooklyn's main large Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Brooklyn "Chinatown, Brooklyn") in and around [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn") are mostly populated by [Fuzhounese speakers](/wiki/Fuzhounese "Fuzhounese"), who often speak Mandarin as well. The Cantonese and Fuzhounese enclaves in New York City are more working class. However, due to the rapid gentrification of Manhattan's Chinatown and with NYC's Cantonese and Fuzhou populations now increasingly shifting to other Chinese enclaves in the [Outer Boroughs of NYC](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"), such as [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), but mainly in Brooklyn's newer Chinatowns, the Cantonese speaking population in NYC is now increasingly concentrated in [Bensonhurst's Little Hong Kong/Guangdong](/wiki/Bensonhurst,_Brooklyn "Bensonhurst, Brooklyn") and [Homecrest's Little Hong Kong/Guangdong](/wiki/Homecrest,_Brooklyn "Homecrest, Brooklyn"). The Fuzhou population of NYC is becoming increasingly concentrated in Brooklyn's Sunset Park, also known as [Little Fuzhou](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_Brooklyn "Chinatowns in Brooklyn"), which is causing the city's growing Cantonese and Fuzhou enclaves to become increasingly distanced and isolated from both each other and other Chinese enclaves in Queens. [Flushing's Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Flushing "Chinatown, Flushing"), which is now the largest Chinatown in the city, and [Elmhurst's smaller Chinatown](/wiki/Elmhurst,_Queens "Elmhurst, Queens") in Queens are very diverse, with large numbers of Mandarin speakers from different regions of China and Taiwan. The Chinatowns of Queens comprise the primary cultural centre for New York City's Chinese population and are more middle class.[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67)

In [Northern California](/wiki/Northern_California "Northern California"), especially the [San Francisco Bay Area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_Area "San Francisco Bay Area"), Cantonese has historically dominated in the Chinatowns of [San Francisco](/wiki/Chinatown,_San_Francisco "Chinatown, San Francisco") and [Oakland](/wiki/Chinatown,_Oakland "Chinatown, Oakland"), as well as the surrounding suburbs and metropolitan area, although since the late 2000s a concentration of Mandarin speakers has formed in [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley"). In contrast, [Southern California](/wiki/Southern_California "Southern California") hosts a much larger Mandarin-speaking population, with Cantonese found in more historical Chinese communities such as that of [Chinatown, Los Angeles](/wiki/Chinatown,_Los_Angeles "Chinatown, Los Angeles"), and older Chinese ethnic enclaves such as [San Gabriel](/wiki/San_Gabriel,_California "San Gabriel, California"), [Rosemead](/wiki/Rosemead,_California "Rosemead, California"), and [Temple City](/wiki/Temple_City,_California "Temple City, California").[[66]](#cite_note-68) Mandarin predominates in much of the [emergent Chinese American enclaves](/wiki/Chinese_enclaves_in_the_San_Gabriel_Valley "Chinese enclaves in the San Gabriel Valley") in eastern Los Angeles County and other areas of the metropolitan region.

While a number of more-established Taiwanese immigrants have learned Cantonese to foster relations with the traditional Cantonese-speaking Chinese American population, more recent arrivals and the larger number of mainland Chinese immigrants have largely continued to use Mandarin as the exclusive variety of Chinese. This has led to a [linguistic discrimination](/wiki/Linguistic_discrimination "Linguistic discrimination") that has also contributed to social conflicts between the two sides, with a growing number of Chinese Americans (including [American-born Chinese](/wiki/American-born_Chinese "American-born Chinese")) of Cantonese background defending the historic Chinese-American culture against the impacts of increasing Mandarin-speaking new arrivals.[[58]](#cite_note-Chee_Beng_Tan_2007_115-60)[[67]](#cite_note-HML-69)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Canada")]

Cantonese is the most common Chinese variety spoken among [Chinese Canadians](/wiki/Chinese_Canadians "Chinese Canadians"). According to the [Canada 2016 census](/wiki/Canada_2016_census "Canada 2016 census"), there were 565,275 Canadian residents who reported Cantonese as their native language. Among the self-reported Cantonese speakers, 44% were born in Hong Kong, 27% were born in Guangdong Province, and 18% were Canadian-born. Cantonese-speakers can be found in every city with a Chinese community. The majority of Cantonese-speakers in Canada live in the [Greater Toronto Area](/wiki/Greater_Toronto_Area "Greater Toronto Area") and [Metro Vancouver](/wiki/Metro_Vancouver "Metro Vancouver"). There are sufficient Cantonese-speakers in Canada that there exist locally produced Cantonese TV and radio programming, such as [Fairchild TV](/wiki/Fairchild_TV "Fairchild TV").

As in the United States, the Chinese Canadian community traces its roots to early immigrants from Guangdong during the latter half of the 19th century.[[68]](#cite_note-70) Later Chinese immigrants came from Hong Kong in two waves, first in the late 1960s to mid 1970s, and again in the 1980s to late 1990s on fears arising from the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests "1989 Tiananmen Square protests") and impending handover to the People's Republic of China. Chinese-speaking immigrants from conflict zones in Southeast Asia, especially Vietnam, arrived as well, beginning in the mid-1970s and were also largely Cantonese-speaking.

### Western Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Western Europe")]

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

The overwhelming majority of Chinese speakers in the United Kingdom use Cantonese, with about 300,000 British people claiming it as their first language.[[69]](#cite_note-71) This is largely due to the presence of [British Hong Kongers](/wiki/Hong_Kong_people_in_the_United_Kingdom "Hong Kong people in the United Kingdom") and the fact that many [British Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese") also have origins in the former British colonies in Southeast Asia of Singapore and Malaysia.

#### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: France")]

Among the [Chinese community in France](/wiki/Chinese_community_in_France "Chinese community in France"), Cantonese is spoken by immigrants who fled the former [French Indochina](/wiki/French_Indochina "French Indochina") (Vietnam, Cambodia and Laos) following the conflicts and communist takeovers in the region during the 1970s. While a slight majority of ethnic Chinese from Indochina speak Teochew at home, knowledge of Cantonese is prevalent due to its historic prestige status in the region and is used for commercial and community purposes between the different Chinese variety groups. As in the United States, there is a divide between Cantonese-speakers and those speaking other mainland Chinese varieties.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

#### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Portugal")]

Cantonese is spoken by ethnic [Chinese in Portugal](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Portugal "Chinese people in Portugal") who [originate from Macau](/wiki/Macanese_people "Macanese people"), the most established Chinese community in the nation with a presence dating back to the 16th century and [Portuguese colonialism](/wiki/Portuguese_Macau "Portuguese Macau"). Since the late-20th century, however, Mandarin- and Wu-speaking migrants from mainland China have outnumbered those from Macau, although Cantonese is still retained among mainstream Chinese community associations.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Australia")]

Cantonese was the dominant Chinese language of the [Chinese Australian](/wiki/Chinese_Australian "Chinese Australian") community from the time the first ethnic Chinese settlers arrived in the 1850s until the mid-2000s, when a heavy increase in immigration from Mandarin-speakers largely from mainland China led to Mandarin surpassing Cantonese as the dominant Chinese dialect spoken. Cantonese is the third most-spoken language in Australia. In the 2011 census, the Australian Bureau of Statistics listed 336,410 and 263,673 speakers of Mandarin and Cantonese, respectively. By 2016, those numbers became 596,711 and 280,943.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

## Cultural role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cultural role")]

See also: [Cantonese culture](/wiki/Cantonese_culture "Cantonese culture") and [Culture of Hong Kong](/wiki/Culture_of_Hong_Kong "Culture of Hong Kong")

*Letter to [the Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_Renzong_of_Song "Emperor Renzong of Song")* by [Su Xun](/wiki/Su_Xun "Su Xun"), [1058](/wiki/1058 "1058"), recited and explained in Cantonese by [Jasper Tsang](/wiki/Jasper_Tsang "Jasper Tsang").

[Spoken Chinese](/wiki/Spoken_Chinese "Spoken Chinese") exhibits a multitude of regional and local varieties, many of which are [mutually unintelligible](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility"). The majority of these varieties are not widely spoken outside of their native regions, although they may be encountered in other parts of the world. Additionally, numerous varieties possess both literary and colloquial readings of Chinese characters for newer standard reading sounds. Since a 1909 decree of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), the government of China has promoted [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") for use in education, the media, and official communications.[[73]](#cite_note-:1-75) However, the proclamation of [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") as the official national language was not fully accepted by the Cantonese authorities in the early 20th century. Proponents of the regional uniqueness of their local language and the commercial importance of the region argued that Cantonese should be preserved. In contrast to other non-Mandarin Chinese varieties, Cantonese persists in a few state [television](/wiki/Television "Television") and [radio broadcasts](/wiki/Radio_broadcasts "Radio broadcasts") today.

Nevertheless, there have been recent efforts to reduce the use of Cantonese in [China](/wiki/China "China"). The most notable of these has been the 2010 proposal put forth by [Guangzhou Television](/wiki/Guangzhou_Television "Guangzhou Television"), which called for an increase in Mandarin broadcasts at the expense of Cantonese programmes. This, however, resulted in protests in Guangzhou, which ultimately dissuaded the authorities from pursuing the proposal. Furthermore, there are reports of students being punished for speaking other [Chinese languages](/wiki/Chinese_languages "Chinese languages") at school, which has led to a reluctance among younger children to communicate in their native languages, including Cantonese.[[73]](#cite_note-:1-75) Such actions have further provoked Cantonese speakers to cherish their linguistic identity in contrast to migrants who have generally arrived from poorer areas of China and largely speak Mandarin or other Chinese languages.[[74]](#cite_note-:2-76)

Due to the linguistic history of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), and the use of Cantonese in many established [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") communities, the use of Cantonese is quite widespread compared to the presence of its speakers residing in China. Cantonese is the predominant Chinese variety spoken in Hong Kong and Macau. In these areas, public discourse takes place almost exclusively in Cantonese, making it the only variety of Chinese other than Mandarin to be used as an official language in the world. Because of their dominance in Chinese diaspora overseas, standard Cantonese and its dialect [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese") are among the most common Chinese languages that one may encounter in the West.

Increasingly since the [1997 handover](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong"), Cantonese has been used as a symbol of local identity in Hong Kong, largely through the [development of democracy in the territory](/wiki/Democratic_development_in_Hong_Kong "Democratic development in Hong Kong")‌ to emphasise a separate Hong Kong identity.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

A similar identity issue exists in the United States, where conflicts have arisen among Chinese-speakers due to a large recent influx of Mandarin-speakers. While older Taiwanese immigrants have learned Cantonese to foster integration within the traditional Chinese American populations, more recent arrivals from the mainland continue to use Mandarin exclusively. This has contributed to a segregation of communities based on linguistic cleavage. In particular, some Chinese Americans (including [American-born Chinese](/wiki/American-born_Chinese "American-born Chinese")) of Cantonese background emphasise their non-mainland origins (e.g. Hong Kong, Macau, Vietnam, etc.) to assert their identity in the face of new waves of immigration.[[58]](#cite_note-Chee_Beng_Tan_2007_115-60)[[67]](#cite_note-HML-69)

Along with Mandarin and [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), Cantonese has its own popular music, [Cantopop](/wiki/Cantopop "Cantopop"), which is the predominant genre in Hong Kong. Many artists from the mainland and Taiwan have learned Cantonese to break into the market.[[76]](#cite_note-Donald-78) Popular native Mandarin-speaking singers, including [Faye Wong](/wiki/Faye_Wong "Faye Wong"), [Eric Moo](/wiki/Eric_Moo "Eric Moo"), and singers from Taiwan, have been trained in Cantonese to add "Hong Kong-ness" to their performances.[[76]](#cite_note-Donald-78)

Cantonese films date to the early days of Chinese cinema, and the first Cantonese talkie, *[White Gold Dragon](/w/index.php?title=White_Gold_Dragon&action=edit&redlink=1 "White Gold Dragon (page does not exist)") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E7%99%BD%E9%87%91%E9%BE%99_(%E7%94%B5%E5%BD%B1) "zh:白金龙 (电影)")]*, was made in 1932 by the [Tianyi Film Company](/wiki/Tianyi_Film_Company "Tianyi Film Company").[[77]](#cite_note-79) Despite a ban on Cantonese films by the [Nanjing authority](/wiki/Nanjing_decade "Nanjing decade") in the 1930s, Cantonese film production continued in Hong Kong which was then under British colonial rule.[[78]](#cite_note-Cantonese_film-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81) From the mid-1970s to the 1990s, [Cantonese films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong") made in Hong Kong were very popular in the Chinese speaking world.

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Cantonese phonology](/wiki/Cantonese_phonology "Cantonese phonology")

See also: [Hong Kong Cantonese](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cantonese "Hong Kong Cantonese")

### Initials and finals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Initials and finals")]

The de facto standard for Cantonese pronunciation is that of Canton ([Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou")). While there are some minor phonological variations between [Hong Kong Cantonese](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Cantonese "Hong Kong Cantonese") and standard Guangzhou Cantonese, the two forms are almost identical.

The phonemic systems of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and Macau exhibit a tendency to merge certain phoneme pairs. Despite the fact that this phenomenon has been described as a lazy sound/pronunciation (懶音) furthermore, this pronunciation is regarded as inferior to that of Guangzhou, and has been prevalent in the territories since the early 20th century. The most notable difference between Hong Kong and Guangzhou pronunciation is substituting liquid nasal /l/ for nasal initial /n/ in many words.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthewsYip20114-82) An example is manifested in the word for you ([你](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BD%A0#Chinese "wikt:你")), pronounced as [nei˩˧] in Guangzhou and as [lei˩˧] in Hong Kong.

Another key feature of Hong Kong Cantonese is the two syllabic nasals /ŋ̍˨˩/ and /m̩˨˩/ merging. This can be exemplified in the elimination of the contrast of sounds between 吳 ([Ng](/wiki/Ng_(surname) "Ng (surname)"), a surname) ([ŋ̍˨˩] in Guangzhou pronunciation) and [唔](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%94%94#Chinese "wikt:唔") (not) ([m̩˨˩] in Guangzhou pronunciation). Hong Kong Cantonese pronounce both words as the latter.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthewsYip201137-83)

Lastly, the initials /kʷ/ and /kʷʰ/ are merging into /k/ and /kʰ/ when followed by /ɔː/. An example is in the word for country (國), pronounced in standard Guangzhou as *[kʷɔk]* but as *[kɔk]* with the merge. Unlike the above two differences, this merge is alongside the standard pronunciation in Hong Kong rather than being replaced. Educated speakers often stick to the standard pronunciation but can exemplify the merged pronunciation in casual speech. In contrast, less educated speakers pronounce the merge more frequently.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatthewsYip201137-83)

Less prevalent, but still notable differences found among a number of Hong Kong speakers include:

* Merging /ŋ/ initial into null initial
* Merging /ŋ/ and /k/ codas into /n/ and /t/ codas respectively, eliminating contrast between these pairs of finals (except after /e/ and /o/[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]): /aːn/-/aːŋ/, /aːt/-/aːk/, /ɐn/-/ɐŋ/, /ɐt/-/ɐk/, /ɔːn/-/ɔːŋ/ and /ɔːt/-/ɔːk/.
* Merging the rising tones (陰上 2nd and 陽上 5th).[[82]](#cite_note-84)

Cantonese vowels tend to be traced further back to [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") than their Mandarin analogues, such as M. /aɪ/ vs. C. /ɔːi/; M. /i/ vs. C. /ɐi/; M. /ɤ/ vs. C. /ɔː/; M. /ɑʊ/ vs. C. /ou/ etc.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] For consonants, some differences include M. /ɕ, tɕ, tɕʰ/ vs. C. /h, k, kʰ/; M. /ʐ/ vs. C. /j/; and a greater syllable coda diversity in Cantonese (like syllables ending in *-p*, *-t* or *-k*).

### Tones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tones")]

Generally speaking, Cantonese is a [tonal language](/wiki/Tonal_language "Tonal language") with six phonetic tones, two more than the four in Standard Chinese Mandarin. This makes Cantonese in general harder to master due to required ability of users to readily be able to process two additional phonetic tones. People who grew up using Cantonese tones can usually hear the tonal differences with no problem, but adults who were brought up speaking non-tonal languages like English and most Western European languages may not be able to distinguish the tonal differences quick enough to optimally use the language. This difficulty also applies to tonal language speakers with fewer tones attempting to master languages with more tones such as Mandarin natives trying to learn spoken Cantonese as adults.

Historically, finals that end in a [stop consonant](/wiki/Stop_consonant "Stop consonant") were considered as "[checked tones](/wiki/Checked_tone "Checked tone")" and treated separately by [diachronic](/wiki/Historical_linguistics "Historical linguistics") convention, identifying Cantonese with nine tones (九声六调). However, these are seldom counted as [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") tones in modern linguistics, which prefer to analyse them as [conditioned](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") by the following consonant.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

As Cantonese is predominantly utilised in Hong Kong, Macau, and other overseas Chinese communities, it is typically inscribed with traditional Chinese characters. Nevertheless, it incorporates supplementary characters and characters with disparate meanings from written vernacular Chinese, due to the presence of lexical items that are either absent from standard Chinese or correspond with spoken Cantonese. This written Cantonese system frequently manifests in colloquial contexts, such as entertainment magazines, social media, and advertisements.

In contrast, formal literature, professional and government documents, television and movie subtitles, and news media continue to use standard written Chinese. Nevertheless, colloquial characters may be present in formal written communications such as legal testimonies and newspapers when an individual is being quoted, rather than paraphrasing spoken Cantonese into standard written Chinese.

## Romanisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Romanisation")]

Systems of Cantonese romanisation are based on the accents of Canton and Hong Kong and have helped define the concept of Standard Cantonese. The major systems are (in order of their invention from newest to oldest): [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping"), the Chinese government's [Guangdong Romanisation](/wiki/Guangdong_Romanization "Guangdong Romanization"), [Yale](/wiki/Yale_Romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale Romanization of Cantonese"), [Meyer–Wempe](/wiki/Meyer%E2%80%93Wempe "Meyer–Wempe"), and [Standard Romanisation](/wiki/Standard_Romanization_(Cantonese) "Standard Romanization (Cantonese)"). Jyutping and Yale are the two most used and taught systems today in the West,[[73]](#cite_note-:1-75) and they do not differ greatly from one another except in how they mark tones. Additionally, Hong Kong linguist [Sidney Lau](/wiki/Sidney_Lau "Sidney Lau") [modified the Yale system](/wiki/Sidney_Lau_romanisation "Sidney Lau romanisation") for his popular Cantonese-as-a-second-language course, and his variant is another system in use today.

[Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Government_Cantonese_Romanisation "Hong Kong Government Cantonese Romanisation")'s and [Macau](/wiki/Macau_Government_Cantonese_Romanization "Macau Government Cantonese Romanization")'s governments use systems of romanisation for proper names and geographic locations, but they transcribe some sounds inconsistently. These systems are not taught in schools. Macau's system differs slightly from Hong Kong's in that the spellings are influenced by [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") due to colonial history. For example, while many words in Macau's system are the same as Hong Kong's (e.g. surnames *Lam* 林, *Chan* 陳), instances of the letter ⟨u⟩ under Hong Kong's system are often replaced by ⟨o⟩ in Macau's (e.g. *Chau* vs. *Chao* 周, *Leung* vs *Leong* 梁). Neither the spellings of Hong Kong's system nor of Macau's look very similar to mainland China's system called [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"), chiefly because it distinguishes between Mandarin's two series of stops while they, although the pronunciation of Standard Cantonese's two series is similar to the Mandarin, do not generally distinguish them, they thus rendering not only /pʰ/, /tʰ/, and so forth, but also /p/, /t/, and the remaining non-aspirates, by the simple spellings ⟨p⟩, ⟨t⟩, etc. vs. it rendering the latter series by ⟨b⟩, ⟨d⟩, and the like.

### Early Western efforts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Early Western efforts")]

Systematic efforts to develop an alphabetic representation of Cantonese began with Protestant missionaries arriving in China early in the nineteenth century. Romanization was considered both a tool to help new missionaries learn the variety more easily and a quick route for the unlettered to achieve gospel-literacy. Earlier Catholic missionaries, mostly Portuguese, had developed romanisation-schemes for the pronunciation current in the court and capital city of China but made few efforts to romanize other varieties.

[Robert Morrison](/wiki/Robert_Morrison_(missionary) "Robert Morrison (missionary)"), the first [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") missionary in China, published a *Vocabulary of the Canton Dialect* (1828) with a rather unsystematic romanized pronunciation. [Elijah Coleman Bridgman](/wiki/Elijah_Coleman_Bridgman "Elijah Coleman Bridgman") and [Samuel Wells Williams](/wiki/Samuel_Wells_Williams "Samuel Wells Williams") in their *Chinese Chrestomathy in the Canton Dialect* (1841) were the progenitors of a long-lived lineage of related romanisations that with minor variations are embodied in the works of [James Dyer Ball](/wiki/James_Dyer_Ball "James Dyer Ball"), [Ernst Johann Eitel](/wiki/Ernst_Johann_Eitel "Ernst Johann Eitel"), and [Immanuel Gottlieb Genähr](/w/index.php?title=Immanuel_Gottlieb_Gen%C3%A4hr&action=edit&redlink=1 "Immanuel Gottlieb Genähr (page does not exist)") (1910). Bridgman and Williams based their system on the phonetic alphabet and diacritics proposed by [Sir William Jones](/wiki/William_Jones_(philologist) "William Jones (philologist)") for South Asian languages.

Their system of romanisation embodied the phonological system of a local-dialect rhyme-dictionary, the *Fenyun cuoyao*, which was widely used and easily obtainable at the time and is still available today. Samuel Wells Willams' *Tonic Dictionary of the Chinese Language in the Canton Dialect* (*Yinghua fenyun cuoyao*, 1856) is an alphabetic rearrangement, translation, and annotation of this *Fenyun*. To adapt the system to the needs of users in an era when there was no standard, but rather only a range of local variants—although the speech of the western suburbs (Xiguan 西關) of Guangzhou was the prestige variety—Williams suggested that users learn and follow their teacher's pronunciation of his chart of Cantonese syllables. It was apparently Bridgman's innovation to mark the tones with (for the upper-register ones) open circles vs. (for the lower-register tones) underlined open circles, in either case at the four corners of the romanised word in analogy with the traditional Chinese system of marking the tone of a character with a circle (lower left for "even", upper left for "rising", upper right for "going", and lower right for "entering" tones).

[John Chalmers](/wiki/John_Chalmers_(missionary) "John Chalmers (missionary)") in his *English and Cantonese Pocket-Dictionary* (1859) simplified the tone-markings by using: a syllable-final acute accent to mark "rising" tones, a syllable-final grave one to mark the "going" tones, no diacritic for the "even" tones, and italics (or in hand-written work underlining) to mark tones as belonging to the upper register. "Entering" tones could be distinguished by the consonants with which they end (p/t/k). [Nicholas Belfeld Dennys](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Belfeld_Dennys&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nicholas Belfeld Dennys (page does not exist)") used Chalmers' romanisation in his primer. This method of marking tones was in most of its details used later also by the [Yale romanization](/wiki/Cantonese_Yale "Cantonese Yale"), where however, importantly, instead of the upper-register tones being marked by italics, the lower-register ones are marked by an 'h' (which comes after whatever letter spells the last vocalic element in the syllable). Another innovation of Chalmers in this dictionary was to eliminate acute/grave accents on top of vowels by adding more distinctions of vowel-spelling (e.g. a/aa, o/oh), so that the presence vs. absence of an accent over the vowel was no longer needed to distinguish different pronunciations of it.

This new style of romanisation still embodied the phonology of the *Fenyun*, and the name of [Tipson](/wiki/Ernest_Tipson "Ernest Tipson") is associated with its particular variety that then was fixed upon by his missionary peers to become [Standard Romanization](/wiki/Standard_Romanization_(Cantonese) "Standard Romanization (Cantonese)"). This was the system used importantly: (with virtually no deviation) by [Meyer-Wempe](/wiki/Meyer-Wempe "Meyer-Wempe")'s dictionary, (even more faithfully) by Cowles' dictionaries (of 1917 & 1965), by O'Melia's textbook, and by many other works in the first two thirds of the twentieth century. It reigned without serious challenge as the standard spelling until Yale's system was devised and became an important rival to it.

The major linguist [Y. R. Chao](/wiki/Yuen_Ren_Chao "Yuen Ren Chao") developed a Cantonese adaptation of his [Gwoyeu Romatzyh](/wiki/Gwoyeu_Romatzyh "Gwoyeu Romatzyh") system. It was first used in Chao's *Cantonese Primer*, published in 1947 by Harvard University Press (which then in 1948, changed by him very little beyond swapping in of Pekingese for the Cantonese, became his *Mandarin Primer*, published by the same Press). The system was then modified by K. M. A. Barnett in 1950 into the [Barnett-Chao](/wiki/Barnett-Chao "Barnett-Chao") romanization system.[[85]](#cite_note-87) The B–C system was used in a handful of texts, including textbooks published by the Hong Kong government, such as *Cantonese Conversation Grammar*, published in 1963.

### Cantonese romanisation in Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cantonese romanisation in Hong Kong")]

See also: [Hong Kong Government Cantonese Romanisation](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Government_Cantonese_Romanisation "Hong Kong Government Cantonese Romanisation")

An influential work on Cantonese, *[A Chinese Syllabary Pronounced According to the Dialect of Canton](/wiki/A_Chinese_Syllabary_Pronounced_According_to_the_Dialect_of_Canton "A Chinese Syllabary Pronounced According to the Dialect of Canton"),* written by [Wong Shik Ling](/wiki/Wong_Shik_Ling "Wong Shik Ling"), was published in 1941. He devised an [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet")-based system of transcription, the [S. L. Wong system](/wiki/S._L._Wong_(phonetic_symbols) "S. L. Wong (phonetic symbols)"), used by many Chinese dictionaries later published in Hong Kong. Although Wong also devised a romanisation-scheme, likewise known as the [S. L. Wong system](/wiki/S._L._Wong_(romanisation) "S. L. Wong (romanisation)"), the latter is not as widely used as his transcription. This system succeeded the [Barnett–Chao](/wiki/Barnett%E2%80%93Chao "Barnett–Chao") system as being the one used by the Hong Kong Government Language School.

The [Linguistic Society of Hong Kong](/wiki/Linguistic_Society_of_Hong_Kong "Linguistic Society of Hong Kong") (LSHK) has advocated the romanisation it devised (and named [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping "Jyutping")). An arguable advantage of it is that its particular use of J for instance (as at the beginning of its name) matches IPA in contrast to most other systems' using Y, resembling English. Some effort has been made to promote Jyutping, but it has yet to be examined how successfully this has caused use of it to proliferate in the region.

Another popular scheme is [ILE romanisation](/wiki/ILE_romanization_of_Cantonese "ILE romanization of Cantonese"), the only system of romanisation accepted by the [Hong Kong Education and Manpower Bureau](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Education_and_Manpower_Bureau "Hong Kong Education and Manpower Bureau") and [Hong Kong Examinations and Assessment Authority](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Examinations_and_Assessment_Authority "Hong Kong Examinations and Assessment Authority"). Books and studies for teachers and students in primary and secondary schools usually use this scheme, but some teachers and students use S.L. Wong's system of transcription.

Those learning Cantonese may feel frustrated that, despite efforts to standardise Cantonese romanisations, most native Cantonese speakers, regardless of their level of education, are unfamiliar with any romanisation beyond the conventional, Latin-letter spellings of Cantonese names. Because Cantonese is primarily a spoken language, meaning that its speakers do not in most genres of writing use its own writing-system (instead, in most of their writing, despite having some Chinese characters unique to Cantonese, primarily following modern standard Chinese, which is closely tied to Mandarin), therefore, it is not taught in schools.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As a result, locals do not learn any of these systems. In contrast to the general use of romanisation in Mandarin-speaking areas of China, systems of romanisation for Cantonese are excluded from the educational systems of both Hong Kong and the province of Guangdong. In practice, Hong Kong follows a loose, unnamed romanisation-scheme used by the [Government of Hong Kong](/wiki/Government_of_Hong_Kong "Government of Hong Kong").

Google's Cantonese input uses Yale, Jyutping, or Cantonese Pinyin (ILE romanisation), the Yale being the first standard.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cantonese&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** De facto spoken language—while no specific variety of Chinese is official in Hong Kong, Cantonese is the predominant spoken form and the de facto regional spoken standard. The Hong Kong government promotes trilingualism between Cantonese, Mandarin, and English, especially in public education.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** De facto spoken language—while no specific variety of Chinese is official in Macau, Cantonese is the predominant spoken form and the de facto regional spoken standard. The Macau government promotes each of Cantonese, Mandarin, Portuguese, and English, especially in public education.
